_comment: The procedure code MP034A is the HBP 2.0 code, replacing HBP 1.0 code M200019 for the same procedure.
clinical_indications: Genetic etiology suspected in any child with at least 1 major or more than 2 minor congenital anomalies.
Genetic etiology suspected in any child with growth deficit (short stature/failure to thrive).
Genetic etiology suspected in any child with developmental delay, mental deficit, or developmental regression.
Genetic etiology suspected in any child with failure to develop secondary sexual characteristics.
Genetic etiology suspected in any child with abnormal genitalia.
Genetic etiology suspected in any child who appears 'different'/'unusual'.

preauth.label: Clinical notes including history, evaluation findings, and planned line of management

claim.label: Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) mentioning the treatment details

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes contain detailed history (prenatal, perinatal, maternal medication, family), signs & symptoms, vital monitoring, planned treatment line, and advice for admission?
Clinical presentation and imaging/investigations suggestive of the diagnosis?
Clinical examination suggested a dysmorphic disorder?

cpd_claim.q: Detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details submitted?
Discharge summary includes follow-up advice at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Clinical notes missing detailed history, evaluation findings, or planned line of management at pre-authorization.
Clinical examination or imaging/investigations did not suggest or support a dysmorphic disorder diagnosis.
Mandatory diagnostic reports (e.g., Whole body scanning, DNA/chromosomal assay, Skeletal Survey) not uploaded at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) missing daily vitals or treatment details at claim submission.
Discharge summary does not include follow-up advice.

additional_information.special_empanelment: Care at a Tertiary Hospital, requiring a multidisciplinary approach involving geneticist, relevant subspecialties, social workers, physiotherapist, and speech therapist.
clinical_key_pointers: Congenital anomalies are a significant cause of neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) admissions.
The presence of a particular congenital anomaly may necessitate evaluation for the presence of other specific associated anomalies or genetic syndromes.
An anomaly is a structural defect that deviates from the normal standard and can be categorized as major or minor.
A major anomaly has surgical, medical or cosmetic importance and may be a marker for other occult malformations.
Minor anomalies may indicate genetic syndromes.

Common physical features include birth parameters, aplasia cutis congenita, holoprosencephaly, asymmetric crying facies, preauricular ear tags and pits, cleft lip with or without cleft palate, esophageal atresia/tracheoesophageal fistula, congenital heart defects, ventral wall defects, and polydactyly.
Potential causes include chromosomal aneuploidy, single gene abnormalities, teratogen exposure, birth defects, and environmental factors.

The approach to a dysmorphic child involves suspicion, clinical evaluation (history, physical examination), investigations, analysis, diagnosis, confirmation, intervention (treatment, counseling, prenatal diagnosis), and surveillance & follow-up.

Examination typically includes anthropometry, assessment for specific syndromes (e.g., Prader Willi, Angelman, Smith Magenis, Miller Dieker), and growth monitoring (height, weight, head circumference).
Investigations include general prenatal & perinatal history, skeletal survey, chromosomal, DNA, biochemical assay. ECG/USG can be useful for additional major or minor abnormalities.
Management focuses on appropriate medical/surgical treatment wherever feasible.
surgical_options: Appropriate medical/ surgical management wherever feasible
